stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = \left( [[a]]xy,\; [[b]]x^{2},\; [[c]]z \right) \) and let \( C \) be the boundary of the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \) in the plane \( z=0 \), oriented counterclockwise when viewed from above. Use Stokes' theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
solution: Stokes' theorem gives \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = \iint_S (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k}\,dA \). Here \( (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k} = \partial_x([[b]]x^2) - \partial_y([[a]]xy) = 2([[b]])x - ([[a]])x \); integrating over the rectangle gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the curl subtraction, using \( \partial_y P - \partial_x Q \) instead of \( \partial_x Q - \partial_y P \).
This treats the curl component as the CONSTANT \( 2b-a \), forgetting it varies linearly with \( x \), then multiplies by the area.
This adds the two partials instead of subtracting them, and mishandles the resulting \( x \)-integration.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = \left( [[a]]y,\; [[b]]x,\; [[c]]z \right) \), and let \( C \) be the circle \( x^{2}+y^{2}=[[R]]^{2} \) in the plane \( z=0 \), oriented clockwise when viewed from above. Use Stokes' theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
solution: Stokes' theorem gives \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = \iint_S (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k}\,dA \), with \( (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k}=([[b]])-([[a]]) \) for the STANDARD (counterclockwise) orientation. Since \( C \) here is traversed CLOCKWISE, the value is the negative of that constant times the disk's area \( \pi([[R]])^2 \): \( -\big(([[b]])-([[a]])\big)\pi([[R]])^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to flip the sign for the clockwise orientation, reporting the counterclockwise value instead.
This adds the two partials instead of subtracting them.
This uses the circle's circumference \( 2\pi R \) instead of the disk's area \( \pi R^2 \).
This is half the correct value.
This adds an extra factor of \( \pi \) to the correct answer.
This subtracts an extra factor of \( \pi \) from the correct answer.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = \left( [[a]]y,\; [[b]]x,\; [[c]]z \right) \), and let \( C \) be the boundary of the triangle with vertices \( (0,0,0) \), \( ([[W]],0,0) \), and \( (0,[[H]],0) \), oriented counterclockwise when viewed from above. Use Stokes' theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
solution: Stokes' theorem gives \( \oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = \iint_S (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k}\,dA \), where \( S \) is the triangular region with legs \( [[W]] \) and \( [[H]] \) on the axes. Here \( (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k} = ([[b]]) - ([[a]]) \) is constant, so the integral equals this constant times the triangle's area \( \tfrac12\cdot[[W]]\cdot[[H]] \), giving \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reverses the curl subtraction, using \( \partial_y P - \partial_x Q \).
This adds the two partials instead of subtracting them.
This uses the full rectangle's area \( W\cdot H \) instead of the triangle's area \( \tfrac12 W H \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = \left( [[a]]y,\; [[b]]z,\; [[c]]x \right) \). Let \( S \) be the portion of the plane \( z = [[alpha]]x + [[beta]]y \) lying above the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \), oriented with upward-pointing normal, and let \( C \) be its boundary, oriented consistently with \( S \)'s orientation. Use Stokes' theorem to evaluate \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} \).
solution: Here \( \nabla\times\mathbf{F} = (-([[b]]), -([[c]]), -([[a]])) \) (a constant vector). For the graph \( z=g(x,y)=[[alpha]]x+[[beta]]y \) with upward orientation, \( \mathbf{n}\,dS = (-g_x,-g_y,1)\,dA = (-([[alpha]]), -([[beta]]), 1)\,dA \). So \( (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{n}\,dS = \big[(-([[b]]))(-([[alpha]])) + (-([[c]]))(-([[beta]])) + (-([[a]]))(1)\big]\,dA = \big[([[b]])([[alpha]]) + ([[c]])([[beta]]) - ([[a]])\big]\,dA \), a constant. Multiplying by the rectangle's area \( [[W]]\cdot[[H]] \) gives \( \displaystyle\oint_C \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{r} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only two of the three curl components, as if \( \mathbf{n}=\mathbf{k} \) on this tilted surface.
This mistakenly treats the curl components as \( (\nabla\times\mathbf{F})\cdot\mathbf{k} = b-a \), the horizontal-plane formula, ignoring the plane's tilt.
This uses only the \( z \)-component of the curl, \( -a \), forgetting the \( x \)- and \( y \)-components entirely contribute through the tilted normal.
This omits the multiplication by the base rectangle's area \( [[W]]\cdot[[H]] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
